mnemonics.title: Function Signature Components
mnemonics.content: PERS - Parameters (Number, Type, Order), Exact match Preferred, Return type Not part of Signature

mnemonics.title: Resolution Priority Order
mnemonics.content: E.P.S.U - Exact match → Promotion (int to long, float to double) → Standard conversion → User-defined conversion

mnemonics.title: Ambiguity Causes
mnemonics.content: D.A.T.C - Default arguments, Array/Pointer decay, Type conversion ambiguity, const/non-const ambiguity

mnemonics.title: Overloading Requirements
mnemonics.content: N.T.O - Name same, but different Type, number or Order of parameters

acronyms.expansion: Parameter list, Exact match, Return type excluded, Signature components
acronyms.usage: Remembering function signature consists of Parameters, Exact match priority, Return type excluded, and Signature components

acronyms.expansion: Exact match, Promotion, Standard conversion, User-defined conversion
acronyms.usage: Sequence of function overload resolution priorities
Remembering that const vs non-const parameters create different overloaded functions

visualTips.visualization: Draw function as a box with name on top and parameter types as labeled input slots - only slots (parameters), not output (return), matter for overloading
Think of a funnel: Exact match passes through immediately, promotions next, standard conversions slower, user-defined slowest
Imagine two doors labeled 'int' and 'double' both opening to the same hallway - compiler gets confused which door to use
Arrays entering function parameters 'shrink' into pointers like a balloon losing air - int[] becomes int*

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant menu having multiple dishes with same name but different ingredients - 'Chicken Curry' can be spicy, mild, or with coconut based on what you order
Like a person's signature - what matters is not the name (return type) but the specific pattern (parameters) that identifies them
Like finding a matching key for a lock - exact key (exact match) works best, then slightly modified keys (promotions), then keys needing work (standard conversions)
Like asking someone 'Do you want coffee or tea?' and they say 'Yes' - the compiler can't decide which option applies
